input.prompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

input.initialPrompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

data.content:
## AppThemeBinding in .NET MAUI

**XAML approach** — use `AppThemeBinding` to supply values for `Light`, `Dark`, and optionally `Default`:

```xml
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyApp.MainPage"
             BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1E1E1E}">

    <VerticalStackLayout Padding="20" Spacing="16">

        <Label Text="Welcome!"
               FontSize="28"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#000000, Dark=#E0E0E0}" />

        <Label Text="This page follows the system theme."
               FontSize="16"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#444444, Dark=#BBBBBB}" />

        <Button Text="Click Me"
                BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#6200EE, Dark=#BB86FC}"
                TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#000000}" />

    </VerticalStackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

When the OS theme changes, MAUI re-evaluates every `AppThemeBinding` automatically — no event wiring needed.

---

**C# alternative** — use `SetAppTheme<T>()` (extension on `BindableObject`):

```csharp
public class MainPage : ContentPage
{
    public MainPage()
    {
        // Page background
        this.SetAppTheme<Color>(
            BackgroundColorProperty,
            Colors.White,          // light
            Color.FromArgb("#1E1E1E")); // dark

        var title = new Label { Text = "Welcome!", FontSize = 28 };
        title.SetAppTheme<Color>(
            Label.TextColorProperty,
            Colors.Black,
            Color.FromArgb("#E0E0E0"));

        var subtitle = new Label { Text = "This page follows the system theme.", FontSize = 16 };
        subtitle.SetAppTheme<Color>(
            Label.TextColorProperty,
            Color.FromArgb("#444444"),
            Color.FromArgb("#BBBBBB"));

        var button = new Button { Text = "Click Me" };
        button.SetAppTheme<Color>(
            Button.BackgroundColorProperty,
            Color.FromArgb("#6200EE"),
            Color.FromArgb("#BB86FC"));
        button.SetAppTheme<Color>(
            Button.TextColorProperty,
            Colors.White,
            Colors.Black);

        Content = new VerticalStackLayout
        {
            Padding = 20, Spacing = 16,
            Children = { title, subtitle, button }
        };
    }
}
```

---

**Key points:**

| Topic | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Reactivity** | Both approaches respond live to OS theme changes — no restart required. |
| **Resource dictionaries** | For reuse, define colors in `App.xaml` and reference them: `Color="{AppThemeBinding Light={StaticResource LightPrimary}, Dark={StaticResource DarkPrimary}}"` |
| **Force a theme** | Override system detection with `Application.Current.UserAppTheme = AppTheme.Dark;` |
| **Detect current theme** | Read `Application.Current.RequestedTheme` (returns `AppTheme.Light`, `Dark`, or `Unspecified`). |

The XAML markup extension is generally preferred — it's more concise and keeps styling declarative. Use the C# API when building UI programmatically.
